0: Environmental implications of anaerobic digestion for manure management in dairy farms in Mexico: a life cycle perspective
Pasiano Rivas-García, José E. Botello-Álvarez, Joaquim E. Abel Seabra, Arnaldo C. da Silva Walter & Alejandro Estrada-Baltazar

To cite this article: Pasiano Rivas-García, José E. Botello-Álvarez, Joaquim E. Abel Seabra, Arnaldo C. da Silva Walter & Alejandro Estrada-Baltazar (2015) Environmental implications of anaerobic digestion for manure management in dairy farms in Mexico: a life cycle perspective, Environmental Technology, 36:17, 2198-2209, DOI: 10.1080/09593330.2015.1024758

<title>Environmental implications of anaerobic digestion for manure management in dairy farms in Mexico: a life cycle perspective </title>
Pasiano Rivas-García, José E. Botello-Alvarez , Joaquim E. Abel Seabrac, Arnaldo C. da Silva Walterc, and Alejandro Estrada-Baltazar

"Department of Chemical Engineering, Technological Institute of Celaya, Av. Tecnológico y A. García Cubas S/N, C.P. 38010, Celaya, Guanajuato, México; Department of Biochemical Engineering, Technological Institute of Celaya, Av. Tecnológico y A. García Cubas S/N, C.P. 38010, Celaya, Guanajuato, México; 'Faculdade de Engenharia Mecânica, UNICAMP, Rua Mendeleyev 200, Cidade Universitária "Zeferino Vaz", Campinas, SP, 13083-860, Brazil; Brazilian Bioethanol Science and Technology Laboratory (CTBE), CNPEM/ABTLUS, Rua Giuseppe Máximo Scolfaro 10.000, Polo II de Alta Tecnologia, P.O. Box 6170, Campinas, SP, 13083-970, Brazil

The environmental profile of milk production in Mexico was analysed for three manure management scenarios: fertilization (F), anaerobic digestion (AD) and enhanced anaerobic digestion (EAD). The study used the life cycle assessment (LCA) technique, considering a 'cradle-to-gate' approach. The assessment model was constructed using SimaPro LCA software, and the life cycle impact assessment was performed according to the ReCiPe method. Dairy farms with AD and EAD scenarios were found to exhibit, respectively, 12% and 27% less greenhouse gas emissions, 58% and 31% less terrestrial acidification, and 3% and 18% less freshwater eutrophication than the F scenario. A different trend was observed in the damage to resource availability indicator, as the F scenario presented 6% and 22% less damage than the EAD and AD scenarios, respectively. The magnitude of environmental damage from milk production in the three dairy manure manage- ment scenarios, using a general single score indicator, was 0.118, 0.107 and 0.081 Pt/L of milk for the F, AD and EAD scenarios, respectively. These results indicate that manure management systems with anaerobic digestion can improve the environmental profile of each litre of milk produced.
Keywords: dairy farm; manure management; anaerobic digestion; life cycle assessment; biogas

3: Mexico, with a production of approximately 11,000 mil- lion litres of milk in 2011,[1] is the third largest milk producer in Latin America and the Caribbean, after Brazil and Argentina. Together, these three countries account for 66% of the production in this area.[2] The Mexican dairy industry comprises 50.6% intensive systems and 21.3% semi-intensive systems.[3 Milk production in the coun- try is distributed mainly in the states of Jalisco, Coahuila, Durango, Chihuahua, Guanajuato and Veracruz, which account for 60% of the national production.[4]

Intensive dairy production generates a number of envi- ronmental problems due to the emissions of carbon dioxide (CO2), nitrous oxide (N20), methane (CH4), ammonia (NH3) and nitrogen oxide (NOx) during livestock produc- tion, transport of inputs to the farm, and breeding and manure management. Furthermore, these activities cause runoff and leaching of phosphate (PO-), nitrate (NO) and NH3 into surface and ground water. These are some of the most common emissions related to climate change and are responsible for the eutrophication of surface and
ground water, acidification of water and soil, which are the most recurrent issues treated in the literature.[5-12

In recent decades, numerous studies have been devel- oped to identify pollutant emissions hotspots and to provide mitigation options during milk production. One methodological tool that has proven to be very effec- tive is the life cycle assessment (LCA) technique, which is a method for assessing the environmental impacts of products, processes or services by including all life cycle phases.[ Milk LCA studies have found that manure management constitutes the largest portion of the environ- mental burden. Heller and Keoleian [6] found that N2O emissions from manure represent approximately 15% of the total greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. Bartl et al. [5] found that the main contributor to soil and water acid- ification is NH3 emitted from dairy cattle manure and urine, which is consistent with the results of Thomasen et al.,[9] who found that the storage and application of manure as fertilizer contributed to approximately 93% of the acidification impact, mainly from the release of NH3. Likewise, they found that the emissions of CH4 and N2O,

primarily from enteric fermentation and manure manage- ment, accounted for 68% of the total GHG emissions from farming activities. These results are consistent with those reported by Boer, [14] who indicated that in conven- tional farms in Sweden, the Netherlands and Germany, the NH3 emissions, primarily from conventional manure man- agement techniques, constituted 78-97% of the potential acidification impacts. Similarly, Cedeberg and Mattsson [15] indicated that almost 90% of this impact is from similar sources.

Many researchers have focused their efforts on the reduction of environmental burdens from manure manage- ment on dairy farms and have suggested an alternative solution of biogas production from waste. Michel et al. [16] explained how the anaerobic digestion of manure and crop wastes decreased the CO2 eq. emissions by 34% compared with typical waste management techniques. In the same way, Clemens et al. [17] showed that during the summer, dairy manure management systems with biogas produc- tion may reduce GHG emissions by approximately 50% compared with systems in which manure is not treated. Similarly, Amon et al. [11] found that GHG emissions were 59% less with anaerobic digestion than when manure was not treated, and that NH3 emissions were reduced com- pared with other management scenarios. In a broader sense, several studies have recommended biogas production as a sustainable alternative for manure management on dairy farms for the following reasons [7,11,16-20]:

<list>Reduces GHG emissions as well as the emissions, runoff and leaching of nitrogen. 
Can replace fossil fuels with renewable energy from biogas. 
Biogas can provide a source of extra revenue for farmers if it is fed to combined heat and power (CHP) to sell electricity. 
Biofertilizer production. 
Reduces odours and pathogens.</list>

However, there is limited information available to explain how anaerobic digestion techniques for manure management could enhance the environmental profile of integrated milk production. Particularly in Mexico, pub- lished research about these opportunities was not found. A study by the Investment Fund of Risk Sharing (FIRCO) revealed that there are approximately 54 methane reduction projects on dairy farms in Mexico; 94% of these projects employ low-tech methods for biogas production (lagoon digesters). This amount represents only 5% of the dairy farms with the capacity to install manure manage- ment through anaerobic digestion. The same study indi- cated that 47% of these projects were not well designed; furthermore, many of these are improperly operated, SO their performance is poor.[21] In this context, the objec- tive of this research was to use the LCA methodology to

analyse the milk production process in Mexico. Conven- tional manure management systems were compared with currently installed anaerobic digestion systems and to a proposed scenario in which anaerobic digestion was imple- mented with a high level of technological sophistication. The ultimate goal was to better understand how manure management systems could be improved and their potential effects on the environmental profile of the milk produced in Mexico.

Together, these three countries account for 66% of the production in this area.[2] The Mexican dairy industry comprises 50.6% intensive systems and 21.3% semi-intensive systems.[3 Milk production in the coun- try is distributed mainly in the states of Jalisco, Coahuila, Durango, Chihuahua, Guanajuato and Veracruz, which account for 60% of the national production.[4]

Intensive dairy production generates a number of envi- ronmental problems due to the emissions of carbon dioxide (CO2), nitrous oxide (N20), methane (CH4), ammonia (NH3) and nitrogen oxide (NOx) during livestock produc- tion, transport of inputs to the farm, and breeding and manure management. Furthermore, these activities cause runoff and leaching of phosphate (PO-), nitrate (NO) and NH3 into surface and ground water. These are some of the most common emissions related to climate change and are responsible for the eutrophication of surface and
ground water, acidification of water and soil, which are the most recurrent issues treated in the literature.[5-12

In recent decades, numerous studies have been devel- oped to identify pollutant emissions hotspots and to provide mitigation options during milk production. One methodological tool that has proven to be very effec- tive is the life cycle assessment (LCA) technique, which is a method for assessing the environmental impacts of products, processes or services by including all life cycle phases.[ Milk LCA studies have found that manure management constitutes the largest portion of the environ- mental burden. Heller and Keoleian [6] found that N2O emissions from manure represent approximately 15% of the total greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. Bartl et al. [5] found that the main contributor to soil and water acid- ification is NH3 emitted from dairy cattle manure and urine, which is consistent with the results of Thomasen et al.,[9] who found that the storage and application of manure as fertilizer contributed to approximately 93% of the acidification impact, mainly from the release of NH3. Likewise, they found that the emissions of CH4 and N2O,

primarily from enteric fermentation and manure manage- ment, accounted for 68% of the total GHG emissions from farming activities. These results are consistent with those reported by Boer, [14] who indicated that in conven- tional farms in Sweden, the Netherlands and Germany, the NH3 emissions, primarily from conventional manure man- agement techniques, constituted 78-97% of the potential acidification impacts. Similarly, Cedeberg and Mattsson [15] indicated that almost 90% of this impact is from similar sources.

Many researchers have focused their efforts on the reduction of environmental burdens from manure manage- ment on dairy farms and have suggested an alternative solution of biogas production from waste. Michel et al. [16] explained how the anaerobic digestion of manure and crop wastes decreased the CO2 eq. emissions by 34% compared with typical waste management techniques. In the same way, Clemens et al. [17] showed that during the summer, dairy manure management systems with biogas produc- tion may reduce GHG emissions by approximately 50% compared with systems in which manure is not treated. Similarly, Amon et al. [11] found that GHG emissions were 59% less with anaerobic digestion than when manure was not treated, and that NH3 emissions were reduced com- pared with other management scenarios. In a broader sense, several studies have recommended biogas production as a sustainable alternative for manure management on dairy farms for the following reasons [7,11,16-20]:

<list>Reduces GHG emissions as well as the emissions, runoff and leaching of nitrogen. 
Can replace fossil fuels with renewable energy from biogas. 
Biogas can provide a source of extra revenue for farmers if it is fed to combined heat and power (CHP) to sell electricity. 
Biofertilizer production. 
Reduces odours and pathogens.</list>

However, there is limited information available to explain how anaerobic digestion techniques for manure management could enhance the environmental profile of integrated milk production. Particularly in Mexico, pub- lished research about these opportunities was not found. A study by the Investment Fund of Risk Sharing (FIRCO) revealed that there are approximately 54 methane reduction projects on dairy farms in Mexico; 94% of these projects employ low-tech methods for biogas production (lagoon digesters). This amount represents only 5% of the dairy farms with the capacity to install manure manage- ment through anaerobic digestion. The same study indi- cated that 47% of these projects were not well designed; furthermore, many of these are improperly operated, SO their performance is poor.[21] In this context, the objec- tive of this research was to use the LCA methodology to

analyse the milk production process in Mexico. Conven- tional manure management systems were compared with currently installed anaerobic digestion systems and to a proposed scenario in which anaerobic digestion was imple- mented with a high level of technological sophistication. The ultimate goal was to better understand how manure management systems could be improved and their potential effects on the environmental profile of the milk produced in Mexico.
9: The dairy farm model that was selected as a case study was based on information collected from several farms in the principal dairy regions in Mexico. Due to their fea- tures and capabilities, these dairy farms are either already producing biogas or are suitable for incorporating biogas production. The selected farm represents a real case located in the dairy region of the Guanajuato state, and corresponds to an intensive production unit with formal procedures. In the following sections, the milk production is described, from livestock feed production up to breeding.
10: The diet of dairy COWS consists mainly of alfalfa, corn silage and concentrate feed (15, 11.7 and 10 kg DM/day, respectively); the latter is composed mainly of 69% corn silage, 19% ground corn and 12% alfalfa, along with minor amounts of salt and minerals. The tillage practices in alfalfa, corn silage and corn production were obtained from technical reports from the National Institute of Live- stock and Forestry Research, INIFAP.[22] Based on these reports, the yields per hectare, land use, amount of fertil- izer, pesticides, diesel and annual seed use was established.

Using a geographical review of alfalfa and corn silage fields that surround dairy farms, a distance of 12.2 km was determined as the transport distance of this forage to the barn. On the other hand, concentrated food was formulated and packaged in industrial plants, which were located at an average distance of 75 km from the farms.
11: The case study dairy farm includes facilities for breed- ing livestock, fodder storage, a milking equipment room, a milk coolers room and administrative offices. Table 1 shows some of the features of this farm, which has a total of 1020 cattle, which is equivalent to 920 livestock units (LU), nearly 58% of which are dairy cows. Approxi- mately 8 ha of the farm are designated for milk production. As no acreage is available for crops, forage (corn silage and alfalfa) and concentrated food are transported to the farm. The average quantity of milk produced in the farm is 9.3 tons of milk/cow-year, which is similar to the yields

The manure generated on the farm (manure and urine) was analysed under three different management scenarios, which are detailed in the next section.

The manure generated on the farm (manure and urine) was analysed under three different management scenarios, which are detailed in the next section.
13: In the fertilization scenario (F), the manure produced in the barn is placed in piles in an open field and later trans- ported by cattle feed producers to fertilize the corn fields for silage. These producers act as external providers of feed to the farm. Among dairy producers in Mexico, this manure management technique (detailed in Figure 1) is the most common.
14: The AD scenario represents the current state of anaerobic digestion for manure management in Mexican dairy farms. In this scenario (Figure 2), the manure is diluted 1:1 and fed into a digester to generate biogas and bio-fertilizer. The digester is an anaerobic lagoon, without agitation and tem- perature control; it is approximately 6 m deep, contained in a plastic membrane, which forms a dome where the bio- gas is stored. The digester has an average yield of 0.154 m³ biogas/m3 digester-day. The generated biogas flows through a filter to remove impurities and humidity and is subsequently fed to a CHP plant to generate electricity.

The generated sludge (85% TS) is removed from the digester every 60 days on average and is subsequently pumped through a centrifugal decanter where it reaches 22% moisture content (MC); the sludge is then sun-dried to 10% MC, to be packaged and sold as bio-fertilizer. Table 2 shows the main parameters of this process.
15: The EAD scenario (Figure 2) incorporates design improve- ments in the digester and sludge treatment. These improve- ments correspond to techniques that yield more biogas and electricity.

In the EAD scenario, the diluted manure, which was previously heated to 35°C, is fed into a completely mixed isothermal digester; the generated biogas has the same destination as in the AD process. This configu- ration was selected because these designs have higher
Figure 1. Dairy production using fertilization for manure management (F scenario).

Figure 2. Dairy production using anaerobic digestion for manure management. The black lines indicate fluxes of both manure manage- ment scenarios (AD and EAD). The dashed grey line is the electricity flow of the AD scenario, and the grey dotted line is the electricity flow of the EAD scenario.

yields than anaerobic lagoons. According to Cantrell et al. [29], the completely stirred reactors produce 1.0-1.45 m³ biogas/m³ digester-day. In contrast, the yields for anaerobic lagoons loaded with cow manure have been reported as 0.011,[30] 0.03-0.23,[31] 0.061 [32] and 0.03 m³ biogas/m³ digester-day.[33] The main design vari- ables and energy requirements of the digester in the EAD scenario are given in Table 2.

The digester was mathematically modelled using the IWA Anaerobic Digestion Model No. 1 (ADM1).[ The initial concentrations of the substrates and microorgan- isms, the proximate composition analysis of the manure and modelling details were obtained and calculated from [35] (shown in Table S1 of the Supplementary material). The FORTRAN programming code that was used in the validation and in the ADM1 solution was also provided by Rivas-García et al. [35]

The dairy digester effluent with 91.4% MC is pumped through a centrifugal decanter to achieve 70% MC, and then fed to a mechanical drum dryer, where water from the slurry is evaporated to reach 10% MC. Finally, the bio-fertilizer is packaged and sold.

18: <header>2.3. System boundary, LCA model and inventory </header>

Figure 1 shows a common Mexican example of specialized milk production, which includes the following stages: agri- cultural production of livestock feed, transport to the farm, cattle rearing, dairy production and manure management.

The scope of this LCA study covered all steps throughout this production chain, from cradle-to-gate. The milk trans- portation to processing and packaging factories is outside the scope of this analysis. The functional unit was adopted as 1 L of whole milk leaving the farm without any prior industrialization process.

The LCA was carried out following an attributional approach. The assessment model was constructed using the LCA software SimaPro 7.3.3 (PRe Consultants, Amers- foort, the Netherlands). The life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) was performed according to the ReCiPe method. The primary objective of the ReCiPe method is to trans- form a long list of life cycle inventory results into a limited number of indicator scores. These indicator scores express the relative severity of an environmental impact category. The ReCiPe method works with two levels of indicators: 18 midpoint indicators and 3 endpoint indicators. It also generates a single score indicator.[36] Further explanation of the method can be found at the ReCiPe website.

The material requirements, energy products and arable land used for each step in the milk production process chain are given in Table S2 of the Supplementary material. The 'background' data of materials and energy production were taken from the EcoInvent v2.2 database.[31 The emissions related to the embodied energy in machinery and buildings were not accounted for, as this may add unnecessary com- plexity to the analysis, since they represent small shares of total emissions and energy [38,39] The emissions from the 'foreground' systems within the milk produc-

22: The input materials for the production of cattle feed were based on tillage practices proposed by the INI- FAP. The direct emissions of nitrous oxide (N20) from nitrogen fertilization on Mexican soils were evaluated by means of a study about gaseous nitrogen loss by N2 and N2O emissions from different tillage systems and nitro- gen sources. [40] The indirect N2O emissions, emissions of carbon dioxide (CO2) from urea, nitrogen volatiliza- tion in the form of ammonia and nitrogen oxides (N-NH3 and N-NOx), and leaching and runoff as ammonia and dissolved nitrates (N NH3 and N - NO) due to nitro- gen fertilization were estimated based on Chapter 11 of the 2006 IPCC Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas Inventories.[41] Diesel consumption during tillage prac- tices was calculated using the average data given by West and Marland.[42 The engine emissions from agricultural machinery were estimated using emission factors from the GREET1 model.| All pesticides applied to the soil were considered to be an environmental load: 98.5% of them reach the ground, and 1.5% end up in the water. [44] Leaching and runoff of phosphate from phosphate fertilization (P2O5) were estimated to be 1.8% of the total applied phosphorus.[4
23: Trucks carrying corn silage, alfalfa and concentrated feed to the farm have a capacity of 16 tons. The emissions to the atmosphere of this activity were evaluated based on the fuel economy of Mexican trucks, which was estimated based on the kilometres travelled to the farm per trip and by using the emissions factors given by NAFTA.
24: The pollutant emissions from the farm were mainly esti- mated for the following sources: enteric fermentation (CH4 emissions), evaluated using IPCC methods, from Chapter 10 [41] and the emissions from motor vehicles used in livestock breeding, which were assessed based on diesel consumption, using emissions factors from the GREET1 model.[43]
25: In the F scenario, when manure is placed in piles in an open field, there are direct and indirect emissions of N2O, volatilization of NH3 and NOx, CH4 emissions, and leach- ing and runoff of NH3 and NO; these flows were eval- uated using IPCC methodologies, from Chapter 11.[41] The diesel consumption and emissions from the transport of manure to corn silage fields were calculated similarly to the calculations in Section 2.3.2. The emissions due to
fertilization with manure were calculated in the same man- ner as in Section 2.3.1; however, this practice also releases CH4, whose flow was evaluated based on Chapter 11 of the IPCC methodologies.

In the AD and EAD scenarios, there are three primary emissions sources: small equipment used for the main- tenance of process areas, CHP plant operation and bio- fertilizer drying. The first was evaluated using diesel and gasoline emission factors from the GREET1 model,[43] and the second was evaluated using IPCC methodolo- gies from Chapter 2,[41] excluding the CO2 emissions, which were treated as biogenic. Finally, the emissions from sludge drying after centrifugal decanting was different for the two processes: in the AD scenario, solar sludge dry- ing emits nitrogen mainly as N2O (direct and indirect) in addition to NH3 and NOx; these emissions were assessed using the IPCC methodologies from Chapter 10.[41] In the EAD scenario, the sludge was dried in a mechanical drum drier, where temperature and contact surface are high, and a large amount of NH4 is emitted as NH3. In this work, it was assumed that 85% of the available NH+ is emitted as NH3, as suggested by Rehl and Müller.[27] The NH+ concentration in the sludge was simulated in the ADM1 model.
26: In the F scenario, when manure is placed in piles in an open field, there are direct and indirect emissions of N2O, volatilization of NH3 and NOx, CH4 emissions, and leach- ing and runoff of NH3 and NO; these flows were eval- uated using IPCC methodologies, from Chapter 11.[41] The diesel consumption and emissions from the transport of manure to corn silage fields were calculated similarly to the calculations in Section 2.3.2. The emissions due to
fertilization with manure were calculated in the same man- ner as in Section 2.3.1; however, this practice also releases CH4, whose flow was evaluated based on Chapter 11 of the IPCC methodologies.

In the AD and EAD scenarios, there are three primary emissions sources: small equipment used for the main- tenance of process areas, CHP plant operation and bio- fertilizer drying. The first was evaluated using diesel and gasoline emission factors from the GREET1 model,[43] and the second was evaluated using IPCC methodolo- gies from Chapter 2,[41] excluding the CO2 emissions, which were treated as biogenic. Finally, the emissions from sludge drying after centrifugal decanting was different for the two processes: in the AD scenario, solar sludge dry- ing emits nitrogen mainly as N2O (direct and indirect) in addition to NH3 and NOx; these emissions were assessed using the IPCC methodologies from Chapter 10.[41] In the EAD scenario, the sludge was dried in a mechanical drum drier, where temperature and contact surface are high, and a large amount of NH4 is emitted as NH3. In this work, it was assumed that 85% of the available NH+ is emitted as NH3, as suggested by Rehl and Müller.[27] The NH+ concentration in the sludge was simulated in the ADM1 model.
27: In this LCA, the main products are milk and live cattle (newborn calves and dry dairy cows). In previous LCAs of dairy farms, the allocation between milk and meat products has been based on the economic,[12,47,48 mass [47,48] and energy criteria.[6,15,49,50] In this work, two of the analysed manure management scenarios generate other co- products: the AD generates bio-fertilizer, and the EAD generates bio-fertilizer and electricity. In this LCA, the economic-based allocation was used due to the nature of the co-products.

The selling price of milk to producers was obtained by prices and market analyses about dairy in Mexico, per- formed by LACTODATA.[51] The price per kg of live cattle meat was estimated using an overall average selling price from different producers in Mexico.

The price of the bio-fertilizer was estimated by relat- ing the N content to the average selling price per ton of N-fertilizer in the urea and ammonium sulphate in Mexico; these prices were obtained from the Ministry of the Economy.[52 The price of electricity, which is sold to the National Electricity Commission (CFE), was estimated using a methodology from the Ministry of Energy based on the sale and export of renewable energy to the national electricity grid, published in Res- olution Num. RES/370/2012 of the Official Journal of the Federation.[53] Table 3 shows the prices for each of the co- products and their allocation percentage for each manure management scenario.

30: The case study dairy farm produces 0.83 kg of manure/L of milk; if this manure is treated under the F scenario, it replaces 75.3% of the application of synthetic N-fertilizers and 48.2% of P-fertilizers (as P2O5), as given in Table S2 of the Supplementary material. On the other hand, if the manure is treated under the AD scenario, 244 MWh/year of electricity is produced, which supplies 90% of the dairy farm's electricity consumption and produces 836 t/year of bio-fertilizer with 8.4% N content. Finally, if the manure is managed under the EAD scenario, the dairy farm elec- tricity requirements are fully supplied, and 836 ton/year of bio-fertilizer with 10.4% N content is produced along with a surplus of 3078 MWh/year of electricity.

The biogas production in the EAD scenario was mod- elled using ADM1. According to Cantrell et al.,[29] when comparing an anaerobic lagoon to a completely mixed reactor, the productivity is approximately 10 times higher; this can be observed in Table 2 (0.154 VS. 1.478 m³ biogas/m3 digester-day), thereby corroborating the ADM1 simulation results.

Figure 3 shows the LCA results of the three scenarios for manure management using the ReCiPe Midpoint (H) method[36]; these results are based on 1 L of milk and are presented in a comparative way. The figure shows that the environmental impacts of the AD scenario are not always lower than the F scenario; on the other hand, the EAD scenario does not present greater indicators for any of the impacts and is always less than the AD scenario. However, it has greater impacts than the F scenario for some cate- gories (the quantitative results of Figure 3 can be found in Table S3 of the Supplemental material).
In LCA studies of milk production systems and agro- industrial waste treatment with anaerobic digestion, it is
Figure 3. LCIA results of the three manure management scenarios, according to the ReCiPe Midpoint (H) method.

common to find primary impact indicators that quantify GHG emissions, acidification and eutrophication.| [5,9,12, 14,15,27,50,54-56] This analysis focuses mainly on these indicators. Figure 3 shows the qualitative similarity of the three scenarios in terms of climate change, terrestrial acid- ification and freshwater eutrophication indicators, which indicates that AD and its technological enhancement can reduce the environmental impacts of milk production.

In terms of climate change, the EAD scenario has the lowest impact (0.728 kg CO2 eq/L of milk) due to the large amount of electricity and biofertilizer produced. Fur- thermore, manure management in this scenario emits 110 times less GHGs than the F scenario (0.0017 VS. 0.187 kg CO2 eq/L of milk), as well as provides the whole electricity demand of the farm, representing reductions of 0.014 kg CO2 eq/L of milk. The GHG emissions in the AD scenario are higher than in the EAD scenario (0.872 kg CO2 eq/L of milk) because it does not produce sur- plus electricity, but only biofertilizer. Furthermore, manure management in this scenario results in 11 times less GHG emissions than the F scenario. On the other hand, the F scenario, using manure as fertilizer, displaces 56.7 N- fertilizer/year and 7.3 t P-fertilizer/year (as P2O2), which represents a reduction of 0.038 kg CO2 eq/L of milk com- pared with the other scenarios. However, this substitution does not significantly impact the general results, estimated at 0.994 kg CO2 eq/L of milk. For comparison purposes, the GHG emissions from conventional dairy farms have been reported in the range between 0.84 and 1.49 kg CO2 eq/L of milk.[12,54,57-59]
In this study, the terrestrial acidification is mainly caused from atmospheric emissions of NH3 and NOx;

therefore, any activity that reduces these flows will sig- nificantly reduce this impact. As shown in Figure 3, the EAD and AD scenarios exhibit 58% and 31% less acidification than in the F scenario. This difference is mainly due to the release of NH3 and NOx during out- door manure storage in the F scenario (2.6 NH3/year and 54 NOx/year), whereas during the sludge drying process in the AD scenario, 10.7 t NH3/year and 28.9 t are released; in the EAD scenario, 4.6 NH3/year and a relatively negligible amount of NOx are released. Another factor contributing to increase terrestrial acid- ification in the F scenario is the emission factor for N-volatilization (kg [N-NH3 + N-NOx]/kg N-fertilizer), which is twice as high for manure fertilization than with synthetic fertilizers. [41] This increases the emissions of NH3 and NOx by 75% during the cultivation of corn silage.

The main areas of milk production and forage cultiva- tion in Mexico are concentrated in the centre and north, away from the coast; as a result, freshwater eutrophica- tion is considered to be an indicator of eutrophication. In this study, the main source of this impact is the leach- ing of phosphate into groundwater due to the production of P-fertilizers and its runoff and leaching during agri- cultural activities. Figure 3 shows that the AD and EAD scenarios, compared with the F scenario, exhibit 3% and 18% lower indicators, respectively. Conducting an analysis without the allocation of co-products, that is, attributing all impacts to milk production, the value of the eutrophication indicator is basically the same for all scenarios, indicat- ing that these differences are substantially attributable to co-products.
Figure 4. The main emissions into air and water from the three manure management scenarios.

There are other impacts that show remarkable differences between the three scenarios, such as particu- late matter formation and photochemical oxidant formation (Figure 3). Air emissions of NO and NH3 are the main contributors to the formation of particulate matter. The emissions in the F scenario are 28% and 58% higher than in the AD and EAD scenarios, respectively; this is primarily due to the same reasons discussed for the terrestrial acidifi- cation indicator. On the other hand, during photochemical oxidant formation, the main contributors are the air emis- sions of NOx; the F scenario has the greatest impact, which is 30% and 63% higher than in the AD and EAD scenarios, respectively, mainly due to the same causes discussed for the terrestrial acidification indicator.

The AD and EAD scenarios reduce environmental bur- dens in two ways: the production of high value-added co- products (Table 3) and the reduction of pollutant emissions from manure management techniques. Figure 4 shows the differences between the main emissions for each scenario, indicating significant decreases in most of them (mainly nitrogen flows). The exception is for CO2 - the F sce- nario presents 22% and 6% lower emissions than the AD and EAD scenarios, respectively, because of the displace- ment of synthetic fertilizers, which emit large amounts of CO2 during manufacturing. Previous studies have also con- cluded that the production of synthetic N-fertilizer is an important source of CO2 emissions.[9,23,60] The quantita- tive results for Figure 3 and the other emissions values can be obtained from Table S4 in the Supplemental material.

The ReCiPe Midpoint method is comprehensive as it covers 18 environmental impact categories (Figure 3); however, it is difficult to interpret because of the amount and abstract meaning of some of the indicators. One option to facilitate the interpretation of the results is to reduce the number of environmental indicators, although in these cases the uncertainty in the results is usually increased. Therefore, the user can choose between uncertainty in the indicators, and uncertainty in the interpretation of the indicators. [36]
Figure 5 presents the LCA results using the ReCiPe Endpoint (H) method, which groups the results into three
Figure 5. LCIA results for the three manure management sce- narios, according to the ReCiPe Endpoint (H) method.

impact areas to facilitate interpretation: damage to human health, damage to ecosystems and damage to resource availability. [36] The differences on human health category shown in Figure 5 for the three scenarios are caused by pol- lutant emissions from manure management techniques and from the fertilization mode (synthetic or manure), as well as resulting from fossil fuel-based electricity savings in the AD and EAD scenarios (Tables 2 and S2). Therefore, the F scenario would lead to more damage to human health, at levels 21% and 44% higher than in the AD and EAD scenarios, respectively. The main gases that influence this indicator are NOx, N20, NH3, CH4 and CO2.

The causes that contribute more to the ecosystem dam- age indicator include arable land occupation and pesticide use (approximately 67% and 18% for the three scenar- ios); however, the differences shown in Figure 5 are essentially attributable to the environmental burdens from the co-products allocation (electricity and bio-fertilizer). Favourable impacts were also observed from the substi- tution of synthetic fertilizers in the F scenario, but these are counteracted by the high environmental burdens from manure management, which makes this scenario to fea- ture the worst ecosystem damage indicator, with 5% and 20% greater impacts than the AD and EAD scenarios, respectively.

A different trend can be seen in terms of the damage to resource availability, for which the AD and EAD scenar- ios have the greatest indicator due to the use of fossil fuels in the production of synthetic fertilizers. The F scenario causes 22% less damage to resources than the AD scenario, mainly due to the displacement of 56.7 t N-fertilizer/year and 7.3 t P-fertilizer/year (as P2O2) with manure fertilizer. The EAD scenario causes 15% less damage compared with the AD scenario mainly from the surplus electricity pro- duced. Table S4 in the Supplementary material shows the fossil fuel consumption for each scenario.

The ReCiPe Endpoint method allows the production of a single score through the aggregation of the three envi- ronmental damages. The single score results are outlined in Figure 6, clearly showing that the manure manage- ment processes using anaerobic digestion can improve the
Figure 6. Single score indicators, according to the ReCiPe Endpoint (H) method.

environmental profile of milk production. From the per- spective of this study, the manure management processes that use anaerobic digestion, as well as those currently installed in Mexico (AD scenario), reduce environmen- tal impacts by 9% compared with conventional manure management (scenario F). Furthermore, there is the poten- tial for reducing the environmental burdens from existing anaerobic digestion with the employment of more sophis- ticated technologies (EAD scenario) by up to 29% (Figure 6). This reduction of the environmental burdens could be even higher with the adoption of biogas processes with higher productivity, and the co-digestion is an alternative for this. Lusk [61] reported that the productivity of a com- pletely mixed digester that is fed with manure and food waste triples biogas productivity; similarly, Wright et al. [62] found that the co-digestion of manure with waste and digesters of the same type can reach yields of 3.25 m³ biogas/m³ digester-day (70% CH4), which is about three times greater than what was established by Cantrell et al. [29] for anaerobic lagoons. This would represent higher electricity yields, hence leading to reduced environmental burdens.

Although it is true that the use of anaerobic digestion as manure management on dairy farms is an appealing alter- native for reducing environmental burdens,[7,11,17,18] it is also true that there are many technological challenges in the construction and maintenance of these processes: manure presents an imbalance in macronutrients, with a carbon/nitrogen ratio of approximately 9:1 compared with the optimum ratios of 15:1 to 45:1 that are required for successful anaerobic digestion [63]; manure contains a lot of fibrous material that is difficult to digest [64,65]; and dilution tends to present problems related to sedimentation and flotation. In Mexico, these processes currently require high levels of initial investment [66]; and a high level of technical knowledge is required to address the various situations that may arise during the design, construction, startup, operation and control.
A study by FIRCO [21] revealed that there are approximately 54 methane reduction projects on dairy

farms in Mexico, 94% of which utilize anaerobic lagoons. This amount represents only 5% of the dairy farms with the capacity to install manure management using anaero- bic digestion. Of these 54 projects, only 9 have electricity generation equipment (such as in the AD scenario), which is mainly due to their high cost. Beddoes et al. [67] determined that approximately 36% of the total cost was associated with this equipment. The same study tells us that 47% of these projects are not well designed and that many of them are improperly operated, SO their perfor- mance is poor. Moreover, no dairy farms were identified with manure management equivalent to the EAD scenario; this does not mean that these processes are technically and economically unfeasible - the literature reports that they have higher productivity and economic benefits than anaerobic lagoons.[29,67-69]

There are government agencies in Mexico, such as FIRCO, that financially support anaerobic digestion projects on dairy farms with up to 50% of the initial investment (below $ 77,000 USD). FIRCO also provides technical advice during the design, construction and oper- ation of projects similar to the AD scenario. However, to date there is no promotion and diffusion of more sophisti- cated technologies, such as in the EAD scenario, perhaps due to the high initial cost [67] or the lack of technical knowledge about this technology. In Mexico, the research and development of these technologies is in a period of latency compared with European and Asian countries such as China and India.[70 A detailed analysis of the limita- tions, problems and prospects of power-generation projects using biogas from anaerobic digesters can be found in [66,71].

Lusk [61] reported that the productivity of a com- pletely mixed digester that is fed with manure and food waste triples biogas productivity; similarly, Wright et al. [62] found that the co-digestion of manure with waste and digesters of the same type can reach yields of 3.25 m³ biogas/m³ digester-day (70% CH4), which is about three times greater than what was established by Cantrell et al. [29] for anaerobic lagoons. This would represent higher electricity yields, hence leading to reduced environmental burdens.

Although it is true that the use of anaerobic digestion as manure management on dairy farms is an appealing alter- native for reducing environmental burdens,[7,11,17,18] it is also true that there are many technological challenges in the construction and maintenance of these processes: manure presents an imbalance in macronutrients, with a carbon/nitrogen ratio of approximately 9:1 compared with the optimum ratios of 15:1 to 45:1 that are required for successful anaerobic digestion [63]; manure contains a lot of fibrous material that is difficult to digest [64,65]; and dilution tends to present problems related to sedimentation and flotation. In Mexico, these processes currently require high levels of initial investment [66]; and a high level of technical knowledge is required to address the various situations that may arise during the design, construction, startup, operation and control.
A study by FIRCO [21] revealed that there are approximately 54 methane reduction projects on dairy

farms in Mexico, 94% of which utilize anaerobic lagoons. This amount represents only 5% of the dairy farms with the capacity to install manure management using anaero- bic digestion. Of these 54 projects, only 9 have electricity generation equipment (such as in the AD scenario), which is mainly due to their high cost. Beddoes et al. [67] determined that approximately 36% of the total cost was associated with this equipment. The same study tells us that 47% of these projects are not well designed and that many of them are improperly operated, SO their perfor- mance is poor. Moreover, no dairy farms were identified with manure management equivalent to the EAD scenario; this does not mean that these processes are technically and economically unfeasible - the literature reports that they have higher productivity and economic benefits than anaerobic lagoons.[29,67-69]

There are government agencies in Mexico, such as FIRCO, that financially support anaerobic digestion projects on dairy farms with up to 50% of the initial investment (below $ 77,000 USD). FIRCO also provides technical advice during the design, construction and oper- ation of projects similar to the AD scenario. However, to date there is no promotion and diffusion of more sophisti- cated technologies, such as in the EAD scenario, perhaps due to the high initial cost [67] or the lack of technical knowledge about this technology. In Mexico, the research and development of these technologies is in a period of latency compared with European and Asian countries such as China and India.[70 A detailed analysis of the limita- tions, problems and prospects of power-generation projects using biogas from anaerobic digesters can be found in [66,71].
32: The case study dairy farm produces 0.83 kg of manure/L of milk; if this manure is treated under the F scenario, it replaces 75.3% of the application of synthetic N-fertilizers and 48.2% of P-fertilizers (as P2O5), as given in Table S2 of the Supplementary material. On the other hand, if the manure is treated under the AD scenario, 244 MWh/year of electricity is produced, which supplies 90% of the dairy farm's electricity consumption and produces 836 t/year of bio-fertilizer with 8.4% N content. Finally, if the manure is managed under the EAD scenario, the dairy farm elec- tricity requirements are fully supplied, and 836 ton/year of bio-fertilizer with 10.4% N content is produced along with a surplus of 3078 MWh/year of electricity.

The biogas production in the EAD scenario was mod- elled using ADM1. According to Cantrell et al.,[29] when comparing an anaerobic lagoon to a completely mixed reactor, the productivity is approximately 10 times higher; this can be observed in Table 2 (0.154 VS. 1.478 m³ biogas/m3 digester-day), thereby corroborating the ADM1 simulation results.

Figure 3 shows the LCA results of the three scenarios for manure management using the ReCiPe Midpoint (H) method[36]; these results are based on 1 L of milk and are presented in a comparative way. The figure shows that the environmental impacts of the AD scenario are not always lower than the F scenario; on the other hand, the EAD scenario does not present greater indicators for any of the impacts and is always less than the AD scenario. However, it has greater impacts than the F scenario for some cate- gories (the quantitative results of Figure 3 can be found in Table S3 of the Supplemental material).
In LCA studies of milk production systems and agro- industrial waste treatment with anaerobic digestion, it is
Figure 3. LCIA results of the three manure management scenarios, according to the ReCiPe Midpoint (H) method.

common to find primary impact indicators that quantify GHG emissions, acidification and eutrophication.| [5,9,12, 14,15,27,50,54-56] This analysis focuses mainly on these indicators. Figure 3 shows the qualitative similarity of the three scenarios in terms of climate change, terrestrial acid- ification and freshwater eutrophication indicators, which indicates that AD and its technological enhancement can reduce the environmental impacts of milk production.

In terms of climate change, the EAD scenario has the lowest impact (0.728 kg CO2 eq/L of milk) due to the large amount of electricity and biofertilizer produced. Fur- thermore, manure management in this scenario emits 110 times less GHGs than the F scenario (0.0017 VS. 0.187 kg CO2 eq/L of milk), as well as provides the whole electricity demand of the farm, representing reductions of 0.014 kg CO2 eq/L of milk. The GHG emissions in the AD scenario are higher than in the EAD scenario (0.872 kg CO2 eq/L of milk) because it does not produce sur- plus electricity, but only biofertilizer. Furthermore, manure management in this scenario results in 11 times less GHG emissions than the F scenario. On the other hand, the F scenario, using manure as fertilizer, displaces 56.7 N- fertilizer/year and 7.3 t P-fertilizer/year (as P2O2), which represents a reduction of 0.038 kg CO2 eq/L of milk com- pared with the other scenarios. However, this substitution does not significantly impact the general results, estimated at 0.994 kg CO2 eq/L of milk. For comparison purposes, the GHG emissions from conventional dairy farms have been reported in the range between 0.84 and 1.49 kg CO2 eq/L of milk.[12,54,57-59]
In this study, the terrestrial acidification is mainly caused from atmospheric emissions of NH3 and NOx;

therefore, any activity that reduces these flows will sig- nificantly reduce this impact. As shown in Figure 3, the EAD and AD scenarios exhibit 58% and 31% less acidification than in the F scenario. This difference is mainly due to the release of NH3 and NOx during out- door manure storage in the F scenario (2.6 NH3/year and 54 NOx/year), whereas during the sludge drying process in the AD scenario, 10.7 t NH3/year and 28.9 t are released; in the EAD scenario, 4.6 NH3/year and a relatively negligible amount of NOx are released. Another factor contributing to increase terrestrial acid- ification in the F scenario is the emission factor for N-volatilization (kg [N-NH3 + N-NOx]/kg N-fertilizer), which is twice as high for manure fertilization than with synthetic fertilizers. [41] This increases the emissions of NH3 and NOx by 75% during the cultivation of corn silage.

The main areas of milk production and forage cultiva- tion in Mexico are concentrated in the centre and north, away from the coast; as a result, freshwater eutrophica- tion is considered to be an indicator of eutrophication. In this study, the main source of this impact is the leach- ing of phosphate into groundwater due to the production of P-fertilizers and its runoff and leaching during agri- cultural activities. Figure 3 shows that the AD and EAD scenarios, compared with the F scenario, exhibit 3% and 18% lower indicators, respectively. Conducting an analysis without the allocation of co-products, that is, attributing all impacts to milk production, the value of the eutrophication indicator is basically the same for all scenarios, indicat- ing that these differences are substantially attributable to co-products.
Figure 4. The main emissions into air and water from the three manure management scenarios.

There are other impacts that show remarkable differences between the three scenarios, such as particu- late matter formation and photochemical oxidant formation (Figure 3). Air emissions of NO and NH3 are the main contributors to the formation of particulate matter. The emissions in the F scenario are 28% and 58% higher than in the AD and EAD scenarios, respectively; this is primarily due to the same reasons discussed for the terrestrial acidifi- cation indicator. On the other hand, during photochemical oxidant formation, the main contributors are the air emis- sions of NOx; the F scenario has the greatest impact, which is 30% and 63% higher than in the AD and EAD scenarios, respectively, mainly due to the same causes discussed for the terrestrial acidification indicator.

The AD and EAD scenarios reduce environmental bur- dens in two ways: the production of high value-added co- products (Table 3) and the reduction of pollutant emissions from manure management techniques. Figure 4 shows the differences between the main emissions for each scenario, indicating significant decreases in most of them (mainly nitrogen flows). The exception is for CO2 - the F sce- nario presents 22% and 6% lower emissions than the AD and EAD scenarios, respectively, because of the displace- ment of synthetic fertilizers, which emit large amounts of CO2 during manufacturing. Previous studies have also con- cluded that the production of synthetic N-fertilizer is an important source of CO2 emissions.[9,23,60] The quantita- tive results for Figure 3 and the other emissions values can be obtained from Table S4 in the Supplemental material.

The ReCiPe Midpoint method is comprehensive as it covers 18 environmental impact categories (Figure 3); however, it is difficult to interpret because of the amount and abstract meaning of some of the indicators. One option to facilitate the interpretation of the results is to reduce the number of environmental indicators, although in these cases the uncertainty in the results is usually increased. Therefore, the user can choose between uncertainty in the indicators, and uncertainty in the interpretation of the indicators. [36]
Figure 5 presents the LCA results using the ReCiPe Endpoint (H) method, which groups the results into three
Figure 5. LCIA results for the three manure management sce- narios, according to the ReCiPe Endpoint (H) method.

impact areas to facilitate interpretation: damage to human health, damage to ecosystems and damage to resource availability. [36] The differences on human health category shown in Figure 5 for the three scenarios are caused by pol- lutant emissions from manure management techniques and from the fertilization mode (synthetic or manure), as well as resulting from fossil fuel-based electricity savings in the AD and EAD scenarios (Tables 2 and S2). Therefore, the F scenario would lead to more damage to human health, at levels 21% and 44% higher than in the AD and EAD scenarios, respectively. The main gases that influence this indicator are NOx, N20, NH3, CH4 and CO2.

The causes that contribute more to the ecosystem dam- age indicator include arable land occupation and pesticide use (approximately 67% and 18% for the three scenar- ios); however, the differences shown in Figure 5 are essentially attributable to the environmental burdens from the co-products allocation (electricity and bio-fertilizer). Favourable impacts were also observed from the substi- tution of synthetic fertilizers in the F scenario, but these are counteracted by the high environmental burdens from manure management, which makes this scenario to fea- ture the worst ecosystem damage indicator, with 5% and 20% greater impacts than the AD and EAD scenarios, respectively.

A different trend can be seen in terms of the damage to resource availability, for which the AD and EAD scenar- ios have the greatest indicator due to the use of fossil fuels in the production of synthetic fertilizers. The F scenario causes 22% less damage to resources than the AD scenario, mainly due to the displacement of 56.7 t N-fertilizer/year and 7.3 t P-fertilizer/year (as P2O2) with manure fertilizer. The EAD scenario causes 15% less damage compared with the AD scenario mainly from the surplus electricity pro- duced. Table S4 in the Supplementary material shows the fossil fuel consumption for each scenario.

The ReCiPe Endpoint method allows the production of a single score through the aggregation of the three envi- ronmental damages. The single score results are outlined in Figure 6, clearly showing that the manure manage- ment processes using anaerobic digestion can improve the
Figure 6. Single score indicators, according to the ReCiPe Endpoint (H) method.

environmental profile of milk production. From the per- spective of this study, the manure management processes that use anaerobic digestion, as well as those currently installed in Mexico (AD scenario), reduce environmen- tal impacts by 9% compared with conventional manure management (scenario F). Furthermore, there is the poten- tial for reducing the environmental burdens from existing anaerobic digestion with the employment of more sophis- ticated technologies (EAD scenario) by up to 29% (Figure 6). This reduction of the environmental burdens could be even higher with the adoption of biogas processes with higher productivity, and the co-digestion is an alternative for this. therefore, any activity that reduces these flows will sig- nificantly reduce this impact. As shown in Figure 3, the EAD and AD scenarios exhibit 58% and 31% less acidification than in the F scenario. This difference is mainly due to the release of NH3 and NOx during out- door manure storage in the F scenario (2.6 NH3/year and 54 NOx/year), whereas during the sludge drying process in the AD scenario, 10.7 t NH3/year and 28.9 t are released; in the EAD scenario, 4.6 NH3/year and a relatively negligible amount of NOx are released. Another factor contributing to increase terrestrial acid- ification in the F scenario is the emission factor for N-volatilization (kg [N-NH3 + N-NOx]/kg N-fertilizer), which is twice as high for manure fertilization than with synthetic fertilizers. [41] This increases the emissions of NH3 and NOx by 75% during the cultivation of corn silage.

The main areas of milk production and forage cultiva- tion in Mexico are concentrated in the centre and north, away from the coast; as a result, freshwater eutrophica- tion is considered to be an indicator of eutrophication. In this study, the main source of this impact is the leach- ing of phosphate into groundwater due to the production of P-fertilizers and its runoff and leaching during agri- cultural activities. Figure 3 shows that the AD and EAD scenarios, compared with the F scenario, exhibit 3% and 18% lower indicators, respectively. Conducting an analysis without the allocation of co-products, that is, attributing all impacts to milk production, the value of the eutrophication indicator is basically the same for all scenarios, indicat- ing that these differences are substantially attributable to co-products.
Figure 4. The main emissions into air and water from the three manure management scenarios.

There are other impacts that show remarkable differences between the three scenarios, such as particu- late matter formation and photochemical oxidant formation (Figure 3). Air emissions of NO and NH3 are the main contributors to the formation of particulate matter. The emissions in the F scenario are 28% and 58% higher than in the AD and EAD scenarios, respectively; this is primarily due to the same reasons discussed for the terrestrial acidifi- cation indicator. On the other hand, during photochemical oxidant formation, the main contributors are the air emis- sions of NOx; the F scenario has the greatest impact, which is 30% and 63% higher than in the AD and EAD scenarios, respectively, mainly due to the same causes discussed for the terrestrial acidification indicator.

The AD and EAD scenarios reduce environmental bur- dens in two ways: the production of high value-added co- products (Table 3) and the reduction of pollutant emissions from manure management techniques. Figure 4 shows the differences between the main emissions for each scenario, indicating significant decreases in most of them (mainly nitrogen flows). The exception is for CO2 - the F sce- nario presents 22% and 6% lower emissions than the AD and EAD scenarios, respectively, because of the displace- ment of synthetic fertilizers, which emit large amounts of CO2 during manufacturing. Previous studies have also con- cluded that the production of synthetic N-fertilizer is an important source of CO2 emissions.[9,23,60] The quantita- tive results for Figure 3 and the other emissions values can be obtained from Table S4 in the Supplemental material.

The ReCiPe Midpoint method is comprehensive as it covers 18 environmental impact categories (Figure 3); however, it is difficult to interpret because of the amount and abstract meaning of some of the indicators. One option to facilitate the interpretation of the results is to reduce the number of environmental indicators, although in these cases the uncertainty in the results is usually increased. Therefore, the user can choose between uncertainty in the indicators, and uncertainty in the interpretation of the indicators. [36]
Figure 5 presents the LCA results using the ReCiPe Endpoint (H) method, which groups the results into three
Figure 5. LCIA results for the three manure management sce- narios, according to the ReCiPe Endpoint (H) method.

impact areas to facilitate interpretation: damage to human health, damage to ecosystems and damage to resource availability. [36] The differences on human health category shown in Figure 5 for the three scenarios are caused by pol- lutant emissions from manure management techniques and from the fertilization mode (synthetic or manure), as well as resulting from fossil fuel-based electricity savings in the AD and EAD scenarios (Tables 2 and S2). Therefore, the F scenario would lead to more damage to human health, at levels 21% and 44% higher than in the AD and EAD scenarios, respectively. The main gases that influence this indicator are NOx, N20, NH3, CH4 and CO2.

The causes that contribute more to the ecosystem dam- age indicator include arable land occupation and pesticide use (approximately 67% and 18% for the three scenar- ios); however, the differences shown in Figure 5 are essentially attributable to the environmental burdens from the co-products allocation (electricity and bio-fertilizer). Favourable impacts were also observed from the substi- tution of synthetic fertilizers in the F scenario, but these are counteracted by the high environmental burdens from manure management, which makes this scenario to fea- ture the worst ecosystem damage indicator, with 5% and 20% greater impacts than the AD and EAD scenarios, respectively.

A different trend can be seen in terms of the damage to resource availability, for which the AD and EAD scenar- ios have the greatest indicator due to the use of fossil fuels in the production of synthetic fertilizers. The F scenario causes 22% less damage to resources than the AD scenario, mainly due to the displacement of 56.7 t N-fertilizer/year and 7.3 t P-fertilizer/year (as P2O2) with manure fertilizer. The EAD scenario causes 15% less damage compared with the AD scenario mainly from the surplus electricity pro- duced. Table S4 in the Supplementary material shows the fossil fuel consumption for each scenario.

The ReCiPe Endpoint method allows the production of a single score through the aggregation of the three envi- ronmental damages. The single score results are outlined in Figure 6, clearly showing that the manure manage- ment processes using anaerobic digestion can improve the
Figure 6. Single score indicators, according to the ReCiPe Endpoint (H) method.

environmental profile of milk production. From the per- spective of this study, the manure management processes that use anaerobic digestion, as well as those currently installed in Mexico (AD scenario), reduce environmen- tal impacts by 9% compared with conventional manure management (scenario F). Furthermore, there is the poten- tial for reducing the environmental burdens from existing anaerobic digestion with the employment of more sophis- ticated technologies (EAD scenario) by up to 29% (Figure 6). This reduction of the environmental burdens could be even higher with the adoption of biogas processes with higher productivity, and the co-digestion is an alternative for this. Lusk [61] reported that the productivity of a com- pletely mixed digester that is fed with manure and food waste triples biogas productivity; similarly, Wright et al. [62] found that the co-digestion of manure with waste and digesters of the same type can reach yields of 3.25 m³ biogas/m³ digester-day (70% CH4), which is about three times greater than what was established by Cantrell et al. [29] for anaerobic lagoons. This would represent higher electricity yields, hence leading to reduced environmental burdens.

Although it is true that the use of anaerobic digestion as manure management on dairy farms is an appealing alter- native for reducing environmental burdens,[7,11,17,18] it is also true that there are many technological challenges in the construction and maintenance of these processes: manure presents an imbalance in macronutrients, with a carbon/nitrogen ratio of approximately 9:1 compared with the optimum ratios of 15:1 to 45:1 that are required for successful anaerobic digestion [63]; manure contains a lot of fibrous material that is difficult to digest [64,65]; and dilution tends to present problems related to sedimentation and flotation. In Mexico, these processes currently require high levels of initial investment [66]; and a high level of technical knowledge is required to address the various situations that may arise during the design, construction, startup, operation and control.
A study by FIRCO [21] revealed that there are approximately 54 methane reduction projects on dairy

farms in Mexico, 94% of which utilize anaerobic lagoons. This amount represents only 5% of the dairy farms with the capacity to install manure management using anaero- bic digestion. Of these 54 projects, only 9 have electricity generation equipment (such as in the AD scenario), which is mainly due to their high cost. Beddoes et al. [67] determined that approximately 36% of the total cost was associated with this equipment. The same study tells us that 47% of these projects are not well designed and that many of them are improperly operated, SO their perfor- mance is poor. Moreover, no dairy farms were identified with manure management equivalent to the EAD scenario; this does not mean that these processes are technically and economically unfeasible - the literature reports that they have higher productivity and economic benefits than anaerobic lagoons.[29,67-69]

There are government agencies in Mexico, such as FIRCO, that financially support anaerobic digestion projects on dairy farms with up to 50% of the initial investment (below $ 77,000 USD). FIRCO also provides technical advice during the design, construction and oper- ation of projects similar to the AD scenario. However, to date there is no promotion and diffusion of more sophisti- cated technologies, such as in the EAD scenario, perhaps due to the high initial cost [67] or the lack of technical knowledge about this technology. In Mexico, the research and development of these technologies is in a period of latency compared with European and Asian countries such as China and India.[70 A detailed analysis of the limita- tions, problems and prospects of power-generation projects using biogas from anaerobic digesters can be found in [66,71].
35: The case study dairy farm produces 0.83 kg of manure/L of milk; if this manure is treated under the F scenario, it replaces 75.3% of the application of synthetic N-fertilizers and 48.2% of P-fertilizers (as P2O5), as given in Table S2 of the Supplementary material. On the other hand, if the manure is treated under the AD scenario, 244 MWh/year of electricity is produced, which supplies 90% of the dairy farm's electricity consumption and produces 836 t/year of bio-fertilizer with 8.4% N content. Finally, if the manure is managed under the EAD scenario, the dairy farm elec- tricity requirements are fully supplied, and 836 ton/year of bio-fertilizer with 10.4% N content is produced along with a surplus of 3078 MWh/year of electricity.

The biogas production in the EAD scenario was mod- elled using ADM1. According to Cantrell et al.,[29] when comparing an anaerobic lagoon to a completely mixed reactor, the productivity is approximately 10 times higher; this can be observed in Table 2 (0.154 VS. 1.478 m³ biogas/m3 digester-day), thereby corroborating the ADM1 simulation results.

Figure 3 shows the LCA results of the three scenarios for manure management using the ReCiPe Midpoint (H) method[36]; these results are based on 1 L of milk and are presented in a comparative way. The figure shows that the environmental impacts of the AD scenario are not always lower than the F scenario; on the other hand, the EAD scenario does not present greater indicators for any of the impacts and is always less than the AD scenario. However, it has greater impacts than the F scenario for some cate- gories (the quantitative results of Figure 3 can be found in Table S3 of the Supplemental material).
In LCA studies of milk production systems and agro- industrial waste treatment with anaerobic digestion, it is
Figure 3. LCIA results of the three manure management scenarios, according to the ReCiPe Midpoint (H) method.

common to find primary impact indicators that quantify GHG emissions, acidification and eutrophication.| [5,9,12, 14,15,27,50,54-56] This analysis focuses mainly on these indicators. Figure 3 shows the qualitative similarity of the three scenarios in terms of climate change, terrestrial acid- ification and freshwater eutrophication indicators, which indicates that AD and its technological enhancement can reduce the environmental impacts of milk production.

In terms of climate change, the EAD scenario has the lowest impact (0.728 kg CO2 eq/L of milk) due to the large amount of electricity and biofertilizer produced. Fur- thermore, manure management in this scenario emits 110 times less GHGs than the F scenario (0.0017 VS. 0.187 kg CO2 eq/L of milk), as well as provides the whole electricity demand of the farm, representing reductions of 0.014 kg CO2 eq/L of milk. The GHG emissions in the AD scenario are higher than in the EAD scenario (0.872 kg CO2 eq/L of milk) because it does not produce sur- plus electricity, but only biofertilizer. Furthermore, manure management in this scenario results in 11 times less GHG emissions than the F scenario. On the other hand, the F scenario, using manure as fertilizer, displaces 56.7 N- fertilizer/year and 7.3 t P-fertilizer/year (as P2O2), which represents a reduction of 0.038 kg CO2 eq/L of milk com- pared with the other scenarios. However, this substitution does not significantly impact the general results, estimated at 0.994 kg CO2 eq/L of milk. For comparison purposes, the GHG emissions from conventional dairy farms have been reported in the range between 0.84 and 1.49 kg CO2 eq/L of milk.[12,54,57-59]
